title: Project Overview
description: The README introduces Barstock: a single-counter liquor shop inventory and billing system built for a real shop in Odisha, India. It lays out the tech stack (FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async, Postgres, Alembic, structlog, JWT auth) and the app/ directory layout that mirrors the layers you'll walk through in this tour: models, schemas, api, security, and services.

title: Application Entry Point
description: app/main.py is where the FastAPI app is assembled. The create_app() factory builds the app, the lifespan context manager starts the DB engine and a background low-stock polling task on startup, and every API router (auth, checkout, voids, lots, products, shops, staff, dashboard, users, health) gets mounted here. This file is the highest fan-out node in the codebase — it is the map of everything the system exposes.
languageLesson: FastAPI's app-factory pattern (create_app()) and lifespan context managers let you control startup/shutdown behavior (opening a DB engine, spawning background asyncio tasks) in one place instead of scattering globals.

title: Configuration, Database & Logging Bootstrap
description: Before any request-handling code runs, three foundational modules wire the runtime together. config.py uses pydantic-settings to load JWT secrets, the Postgres DSN, and CORS/job-interval settings from environment variables behind a cached get_settings(). db.py builds the async SQLAlchemy engine, session factory, and declarative Base that every model inherits from. logging_config.py sets up structlog so every layer emits structured JSON logs via a shared get_logger() helper.
languageLesson: pydantic-settings validates environment variables at startup (failing fast on misconfiguration) rather than raising cryptic errors deep inside request handlers. SQLAlchemy 2.0's async engine/session pattern requires explicit session-per-request scoping, which is why db.py exposes a session factory rather than a single global session.

title: Security & Request Dependencies
description: Authentication rests on two small, focused modules: jwt.py issues and decodes JWT access tokens, and passwords.py hashes/verifies credentials with bcrypt. app/api/deps.py ties them into FastAPI's dependency-injection system, providing db_session, get_current_user (decoding the bearer token), require_role (RBAC guard), and resolve_write_shop_id/require_shop_scope (multi-tenant write scoping). Nearly every API router in this project depends on deps.py, making it one of the highest fan-in files in the codebase.
languageLesson: FastAPI dependencies (functions passed to Depends()) let you compose cross-cutting concerns — auth, DB sessions, role checks — as reusable, testable functions instead of decorators or middleware, and each endpoint declares exactly which dependencies it needs.

title: Core Domain: Users & Shops
description: The shop is the tenant boundary in this multi-shop system: models/shop.py defines the Shop entity holding shop code, GSTIN, excise duty rate, invoice numbering state, and low-stock defaults. models/user.py defines the User account and the UserRole enum (superadmin/owner/receiver/cashier) with a relationship back to Shop. Together these two files are the most depended-upon models in the codebase, underpinning every role check and shop-scoped query you'll see in later API and service layers.

title: Inventory & Audit Log Models
description: Building on the Shop/User foundation, product.py defines a Product (a specific brand/bottle-size SKU) with barcode, price, and low-stock threshold. lot.py models stock-receiving lots and their per-product line items — this is how new stock enters the system. log.py defines a shared _BaseLog table with three subtype tables (InvoicingLog, StockinLog, AdminLog) that inherit from it, giving the shop an audit trail of every invoicing, stock-in, and admin action.
languageLesson: SQLAlchemy 2.0's declarative mapping supports single-table inheritance (_BaseLog subclassed by InvoicingLog/StockinLog/AdminLog), letting shared audit-log columns live in one place while each subtype adds its own fields.

title: The Transactional Core: Invoices
description: models/invoice.py is the heart of the billing domain: it defines Invoice, InvoiceLine, Payment, EOD sign-off records, and idempotency keys in one file. This is the second-highest fan-in file in the project — nearly every service and test that touches money or stock ultimately reads or writes through these tables. Notice how idempotency keys and EOD sign-off state are modeled directly alongside the invoice, not bolted on separately, reflecting decisions made early in the project's design (see the harness docs referenced in the README).

title: Service Layer: Checkout & End-of-Day
description: services/checkout.py is the core transaction: it validates cart lines against live stock, enforces idempotency and EOD sign-off rules, and finalizes an invoice with payments — all inside a single database transaction, including protecting the last unit of stock under concurrent requests. services/eod.py implements end-of-day sign-off, computing day totals with payment-mode breakdowns and retrieving sign-off history and the pending void queue. These services keep business rules independent of HTTP concerns, so the same logic is reused by both the API layer and the test suite.
languageLesson: Keeping business logic in a service layer (rather than inline in FastAPI route handlers) makes transaction boundaries explicit and lets you unit-test checkout/EOD rules — including concurrency edge cases like double-selling the last unit — without spinning up HTTP requests.

title: Service Layer: Voids, Low-Stock & Receipts
description: services/voids.py handles invoice voiding: immediate same-day voids, plus a request/approve/reject workflow for post-EOD voids that require owner sign-off and generate reversal entries. services/low_stock.py computes which products are at or below threshold by deriving current stock as received lot quantities minus sold invoice quantities — notice there's no separate 'stock' column; stock is always derived via SQL aggregation over lots and invoices. services/invoice_pdf.py renders a finalized invoice as a printable PDF receipt using ReportLab, pulling in the shop's own tax settings rather than hardcoding them.

title: API Layer: Identity & Shop Management
description: auth.py exposes superadmin and shop-user login, verifying credentials and issuing JWTs. users.py returns the current user's public profile. staff.py lets shop owners create and list staff/receiver/cashier accounts scoped to their own shop. shops.py lets superadmins list all shops and lets owners view/update their own shop's GSTIN, excise rate, and low-stock defaults. All four routers lean on the deps.py guards from Step 4 to enforce who can do what.

title: API Layer: Inventory
description: lots.py exposes endpoints to create, list, and fetch stock-receiving lots, writing stock-in logs and enforcing shop scoping as each lot arrives. products.py handles product management: create, list, barcode lookup, update, and bulk CSV import, all with unique-barcode validation. These two routers are the entry points for the inventory models from Step 6 — every lot created here increases the derived stock computed by low_stock.py in Step 9.

title: API Layer: Checkout, Voids & Dashboard
description: checkout.py handles checkout finalization and invoice retrieval (including PDF export), delegating straight to the checkout service from Step 8 and translating domain errors into HTTP responses. voids.py exposes the immediate and request/approve/reject void workflows from services/voids.py. dashboard.py ties it all together for shop owners: EOD sign-off, day totals, sign-off history, the pending void queue, and low-stock reporting — the single screen an owner checks at the end of the day.

title: Database Schema Evolution
description: alembic/env.py wires the async SQLAlchemy engine and the models from Steps 5-7 into Alembic's offline/online migration runner. The foundation migration creates the core shops, users, and log tables establishing the multi-shop baseline, while the invoices/invoice_lines/payments migration adds the billing tables that back the Invoice model from Step 7. Each migration in alembic/versions/ is a dated, incremental step — run `alembic upgrade head` and the schema evolves alongside the ORM models without ever hand-editing the database.
languageLesson: Alembic migrations should be idempotent and ordered by a revision chain (each file references the prior revision). Splitting schema changes into small, incremental files — as this project does per-feature (foundation, lots, products, invoices, EOD, voids, GSTIN) — keeps the migration history readable and each change independently reversible.

title: Test Suite: Fixtures & Checkout Coverage
description: tests/conftest.py provisions an isolated per-test Postgres database (via Alembic migrations and asyncpg), an async HTTP client wired to the FastAPI app, and role-scoped fixtures for owner/receiver/cashier/superadmin users — every other test file in the suite depends on it. test_checkout.py then exercises the checkout service end-to-end: split payments, idempotency replay, concurrent last-unit stock races, invoice immutability, and PDF generation, proving that the business rules from Step 8 actually hold under real HTTP requests.
languageLesson: Pytest fixtures (like the role-scoped owner/receiver/cashier clients here) are composable and scoped per-test by default, which is what makes it safe to spin up a fresh isolated database per test without state leaking between them.

title: Local Infrastructure & Project Config
description: docker-compose.yml runs a local Postgres 16 container with a health check and named volume, matching the credentials the app expects via DATABASE_URL — this is what conftest.py and the dev server connect to locally. .env.example documents every environment variable (JWT secrets, bcrypt cost, DB DSN, CORS origins) that config.py from Step 3 reads at startup. pyproject.toml declares the full dependency stack, the `barstock` CLI entry point, and tool configuration for ruff, pytest, and coverage — the manifest that ties the whole project together.
languageLesson: docker-compose health checks (used here for Postgres) let dependent services or test harnesses wait for the database to actually accept connections rather than just for the container process to start, avoiding flaky startup races.
